exercises.title: The reality gap
exercises.prompt: Define the reality gap and explain why training RL policies in simulation is necessary despite it. Give three concrete ways simulators differ from reality, and what 'a policy that memorized the simulator' means.
exercises.hint: Reality gap = systematic sim-vs-real distribution difference. Sim is needed: unlimited resets, no hardware damage, parallel, reproducible. Differences: friction/dynamics, sensor noise/lens distortion, motor delays/backlash. Memorizing sim = overfit to sim quirks, fails on hardware.
exercises.solution:
**The reality gap:** the **systematic difference between the simulator's distribution and the real world's distribution** — the simulator is *wrong* in consistent ways, so a policy optimal in sim is not optimal (or even functional) on hardware.

**Why simulation is necessary anyway:** training a real robot directly is **slow, dangerous, and expensive** — a biped needs **millions of episodes** to learn to walk, and a real biped that **falls even once can break hardware**. Simulation gives **unlimited resets, no physical damage, deterministic reproducibility, and massively parallel environments** (thousands of robots at once on GPU sims like Isaac/MJX). You simply can't collect the required experience safely on real hardware, so sim training is unavoidable — the reality gap is the price.

**Three concrete sim-vs-real differences:**
1. **Dynamics / physics:** real **bearings have more friction, joints have backlash, contacts and deformations** behave differently than the idealized rigid-body model (MuJoCo et al.) — mass, friction, and restitution are all approximate.
2. **Sensors:** real **cameras have lens distortion, noise, motion blur, rolling shutter, and lighting variation** the simulator's clean rendering omits; depth/IMU sensors have noise and bias.
3. **Actuation / timing:** real **motors have latency, communication delays, torque saturation, and wear** that most sim models skip, so commands take effect differently than simulated.
(Plus environmental factors: wind, dust, temperature, variable lighting/terrain.)

**'A policy that memorized the simulator':** a policy that **overfit to the simulator's specific quirks** — it learned to exploit the exact (wrong) friction, the exact noise-free images, the exact timing — rather than learning a **robust controller for the underlying task**. It scores beautifully in sim and **fails immediately on hardware** because the quirks it depended on don't exist in reality. The whole field of sim-to-real exists to prevent this: produce policies **robust to the sim-to-real distribution shift**, not policies tuned to one simulator.

exercises.title: Domain randomization
exercises.prompt: Explain domain randomization (DR) as the dominant sim-to-real technique: randomize simulator parameters during training so reality looks like 'just another variation'. Sketch the training loop, give examples of what to randomize (dynamics vs visual), and connect it to the bias-variance / robustness idea.
exercises.hint: Randomize friction, mass, delays, textures, lighting, camera pose each episode. Loop: sample params ~ ranges, run episode, train. If real params fall within the training distribution, the policy generalizes. It's deliberate domain shift to force robustness (like data augmentation).
exercises.solution:
**Domain randomization (DR):** instead of trying to make the simulator *exactly* match reality (impossible), **randomize the simulator's parameters across a wide range during training**, so the policy must work across *many* variations. The bet: if the **real world's parameters fall somewhere inside the randomized training distribution**, then to the trained policy **reality just looks like one more variation it has already seen** — no special adaptation needed. It's the cheapest, most robust, and dominant technique.

**The training loop:**
```python
for episode in range(N):
    params = sample_random_params(ranges)   # new physics+visuals each episode
    env = make_sim(params)                   # e.g. friction, mass, latency, textures
    rollout = run_policy(env, policy)
    update_policy(rollout)                    # PPO etc.
# Deploy the SAME policy on real hardware (real params ∈ training ranges → works)
```
Randomize **per episode** (or per environment in a parallel sim) so the policy sees a huge variety.

**What to randomize:**
- **Dynamics randomization:** friction coefficients, link masses and inertias, joint damping, motor strength, **actuator latency/delays**, control noise, contact stiffness — so the policy is robust to physics it can't model exactly.
- **Visual randomization:** textures, colors, **lighting (direction/intensity)**, camera position/FOV, backgrounds, added image noise/blur — so a vision-based policy is robust to real-camera appearance (famously used to transfer grasping/manipulation from sim).

**Connection to bias-variance / robustness:** DR is essentially **data augmentation for control / deliberate domain shift**. By **injecting variance** into the training distribution (many randomized worlds), you **trade a bit of peak in-sim performance for far better generalization** — the policy can't overfit to any single simulator's quirks because the quirks keep changing, so it learns the **invariant, task-relevant** structure. In bias-variance terms, you accept slightly higher bias (the policy is a generalist, not perfectly tuned to one environment) in exchange for **drastically lower variance under distribution shift** (it doesn't fall apart when the environment differs). Wider randomization → more robust but harder to learn (and possibly more conservative); too narrow → may not cover the real parameters and fails to transfer. Tuning the **randomization ranges** to *contain* reality while staying learnable is the core craft of DR.

exercises.title: DR vs domain adaptation vs system ID, and the 2026 recipe
exercises.prompt: Compare the three sim-to-real approaches — domain randomization, domain adaptation, and system identification — on what each assumes and costs. Explain their failure modes, how the 2026 recipe combines them with massive parallel simulation, and why this matters for the broader trend of training agents (including LLM agents) in simulators/RL gyms.
exercises.hint: DR: no real data, just randomize (may be over-conservative). Domain adaptation: use a little real data to fine-tune/align features (needs real data, can overfit). System ID: measure real params and match sim (accurate but per-robot, brittle to change). 2026: DR + a bit of real-world adaptation + GPU sims (Isaac). Broader: RL gyms/sims for agents.
exercises.solution:
**The three approaches:**
| Approach | Assumes / needs | How it works | Cost / failure mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Domain randomization** | nothing from the real robot | randomize sim params widely so reality ∈ training distribution | **no real data needed, most scalable**; but if ranges are **too wide** → over-conservative/suboptimal policy or hard to learn; **too narrow** → misses real params, fails to transfer |
| **Domain adaptation** | a **little real data** | fine-tune the policy or **align sim/real feature distributions** (e.g. adversarial feature matching, image translation sim→real) using real samples | needs **real-world data collection** (some hardware risk/cost); can **overfit** to the small real dataset; alignment may not capture dynamics gaps |
| **System identification** | ability to **measure the real system** | **estimate the real robot's actual parameters** (friction, mass, delays) and **set the simulator to match** them | most **accurate when correct**, but **per-robot, labor-intensive**, and **brittle**: parameters drift with wear/temperature/payload, so an ID'd sim goes stale; doesn't cover un-modeled effects |

**Their complementary failure modes** are why no single one wins: DR is robust but blunt, adaptation needs scarce real data and can overfit, system ID is precise but fragile and per-unit.

**The 2026 recipe — combine all three with massive parallel simulation:**
- **GPU-accelerated simulators** (Isaac Sim/Isaac Lab, MuJoCo MJX) run **thousands of randomized environments in parallel**, making it cheap to train across enormous parameter variety — DR at scale.
- **DR as the backbone** for broad robustness, **narrowed/centered using system identification** (measure the real robot to set sensible randomization *ranges* around the true parameters — 'randomize *around* reality'), and **a small amount of real-world adaptation/fine-tuning** to close the residual gap.
- Often with **adaptive/automatic DR** (expand ranges as the policy masters them) and **online adaptation** (policies that infer environment parameters from a short history at runtime, e.g. RMA — rapid motor adaptation). This combined recipe is what powers modern legged-robot and manipulation sim-to-real.

**Why this matters beyond robotics (incl. LLM agents):** the deep pattern is **'train in a cheap, safe, parallel simulator/gym, then transfer to the messy real world, with techniques to bridge the distribution gap.'** This generalizes: 
- **LLM/agentic RL** increasingly trains in **simulated environments and RL gyms** — sandboxed tool-use environments, code execution sandboxes, browser/computer-use simulators, game-like task suites (Lesson 12, and agent phases) — because real-world actions (sending emails, executing trades, hitting production APIs) are **slow, costly, and dangerous** (the same reasons you don't train a biped on hardware). 
- The **reality gap reappears as the sim-to-deployment gap**: an agent that overfits to a sandboxed tool API or a synthetic eval may fail on real, noisy production systems — the LLM analog of 'memorized the simulator'. 
- The mitigations rhyme: **randomize the training environments** (diverse tasks, perturbed tools, varied prompts/personas — DR), **fine-tune on a little real interaction data** (adaptation), and **calibrate the sim to real conditions** (system ID). 
So sim-to-real isn't just a robotics topic — it's the **general discipline of making policies trained in cheap proxies survive contact with reality**, which is exactly the challenge of deploying any RL-trained agent, including the LLM agents that dominate 2026.
